speaker: interviewer
text: [? Mansey ?] Rosner. [? Mansey, ?] we're now-- in our story, you talked about how you're in this small town. But eventually, you have to go to the Krakow ghetto, Is that right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ghetto, yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you physically get to the ghetto? And how did you know that you had to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were-- there were signs. There were signs all over, placard, you know, papers all over on the streets. And they let us know. How we got there, we got there by-- by horses and-- and-- and wagons. We got there, and there was the Jewish organization, the Jewish police organization. They gave us rooms in the ghetto. We-- we-- we got the room in the ghetto, one room in a big apartment. There was-- in one room, there was the dental. The-- the-- the owner of the-- of the apartment was a dentist, a Jewish dentist. He wasn't-- not alive anymore, but once it belonged to him.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we got the room there. In this room, we were living-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] me, my husband, and my son, his-- my husband's two sisters, my brother-in-law is the husband of her, of Regina, and her son. We were-- we were living in one room. We had a little iron stove where we cooked. And in-- and in every room were living at least 8 to 10 people, and we had one bathroom. How did you get food every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Black market, black market. We bought it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had to eat food for-- when you had money, you could pay. You know, this black market was blooming.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do during the day? And what did you do with your son during the day as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. Just sitting around. Nothing. We could go out on the street. It was the ghetto. I earned some money there. [CHUCKLES] My brother-in-law had some friends. He used to like to play cards.

speaker: interviewee
text: So at night, after every-- the children went to sleep, they came up in the house. And he was sitting. We playing poker or playing some other games.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then came-- it was 11 o'clock. They got hungry. So I-- during the day, I baked some cake, and in the evening, I served coffee and cake, or I make a sandwich, [NON-ENGLISH]. And [INAUDIBLE] paid me for it. And that's how I-- I made some money.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like in the conditions of the-- the people downstairs, the Jewish police, and the-- the people giving you your orders? What kind of people were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish police, they were picked out-- I tell you the honest truth. I personally had no bad experience with the Jewish police. But there were rumors that some of the Jewish policemen didn't behave the way they should. But I didn't have these experiences.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the conditions progressively get worse in the ghetto, or was-- did it stay about the same for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They got worse, because the people run out of money. It was harder to-- to get the food. It was harder to-- to put the meal on the table, you know? That's how it became worse. No, then-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then came Plaszow, which was in the surroundings of Krakow. We had to get there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you-- you got to Plazsow, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what was a daily routine that you did there in the ghetto? Did you have a certain job that you had to do during the day, or were you just doing nothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, doing nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your husband working?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, he was not working. There was a little, tiny little restaurant, bar, night club, I don't know how to call it, which belonged to a Jewish man which was a-- a spy. And he got the permission to-- to open this-- this bar. And Henry used to play there two, three times a week a little bit, I remember, but for peanuts, I mean, nothing to-- to rave about it. It was very, very, tough, very, hard times. Very hard times. We had to struggle to-- to buy a potato or buy a little piece of meat or something. It wasn't easy. And to-- to-- to go to the bathroom was a-- was a big thing. We had to stay in-- in line, you know, to wash, to bathe, to-- to-- to-- it was-- it was very, very, very tough. It kept you busy all day long, especially when you had children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your son asking you questions about the conditions?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My son found himself always something to play with, you know. This time [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a box, you know, anything, a stick, a box-- from old stockings, you make a ball. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's different. It's very, very different.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in the ghetto? From 1940?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1940 Until about 1942, two years, until about 1942-- 1941, 1942, when we came to Plazsow.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you physically know that you had to go from Krakow to Plazsow? Did they-- did they come and knock on your door? Did they tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I personally-- I-- my-- my husband got up there earlier [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with my boy. I was in the hospital. I was pregnant, and I had a-- a abortion. So I was in a hospital. And I got up there. Luckily, I went out in time, before they liquidated. They killed everybody in the hospital. They liquidated the hospital. Luckily, I got out of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you wind up going to the hospital in the first place?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went. I had to go. Those were in ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go because you were physically ill, or did they make you have an abortion?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. I wanted to have the abortion. No. I-- I didn't want to give-- bear a child in these conditions. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So that's-- I had to go. There was a doctor there, Dr. Sternberg, I remember. And he examined me. And this is-- said-- and that's what's happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did a lot of other women who found themselves pregnant go through that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. I don't think so. This was for me also an accident. We didn't-- it was an accident, you know? I didn't want to become in this situation. But I didn't want to give birth either. I probably would have never lived through anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: So during this time that you were in the hospital, what happened with your son and your-- your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: My son-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my husband had some connection. While he was in the ghetto, there was some some [NON-ENGLISH], you know? You had to work for the Germans, and he was in a-- in a-- he worked for the-- for the air force, for the German air force, for an office. And he got friendly with a-- with a German, with a nice German. And he took my boy. He took him with his wife or his friend, was not his wife, his girlfriend. And he picked him up from the ghetto. And he smuggled him out from the ghetto, and he was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember the German's name, the German who did this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ritschel, Ritschel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: R-I-T-S-C-H-E-L, Ritschel was his name. Herr Ritschel. I didn't know his first name. And he-- he-- he always find out when something is going to happen, some action is going to be in the ghetto, some killing, some shooting, something. And he says it to my husband. And they took the boy out of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now where did they take-- and by the way, what is your son's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Alexander.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they take Alexander?

speaker: interviewee
text: To his house. To his house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he keep Alexander there for the course of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: He keep him there for a week or two until it quiet down, the [NON-ENGLISH], and then he came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you still keep in touch? Or did your husband keep in touch with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. He died. He died. He was an elderly man, and he died.

speaker: interviewer
text: So during this time that you were in the hospital, your son at this point was-- was--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was not in-- he was-- the time when-- when Henry had to go to the-- to the Plazsow, he was not with him. He was with Mr. Ritschel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you were in the hospital, was that the time that your husband went to Plazsow?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Plaszow, yes. But right away, when he was in Plazsow, somehow somebody talked to the commandant of the-- of the-- lager, that he's a violinist. And he happened to love music, this man, this murderer. Amon Goth was his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Amon Goth.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you spell that for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: G-O-T-H, O with two-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] two on top, Goth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he find out that-- and he's-- my husband's brother was also there with the accordion. And they all right away got started to play for him. And that's how he saved-- through this, he saved himself, and me, and my son, and everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you physically get from-- when you went back to the ghetto from the hospital, how did you get to Plazsow yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband came down from-- from Plazsow with a-- with a guard, which Goth gave him the permission. And he took-- he took me out from the hospital, and he brought me up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get your son at the-- at-- from--

speaker: interviewee
text: My son was not there yet. My son was still with Ritschel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your husband-- now, what year is this-- 1942?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was 1942, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what-- what month it was in? It was already summer. It must be summer.

speaker: interviewee
text: So you went Plazsow with your husband. What happened when you got there? When I got there, I was with a group of woman. And came a-- a German soldier. And he said, who speaks German? So all the woman pushed me out, because I didn't speak Polish. I spoke only German.

speaker: interviewee
text: They pushed me out. She speaks German. And so the-- the few other woman spoke German, so we became the-- the block altester, you know? Each of us was-- was assigned to one block. We were 300 woman. And we're supposed to-- each of us supposed to take care of one block. Were 10 blocks like this. What were your job-- what was your job specifically? What kind of functions did you have to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to see that the block was clean, that we-- we-- all 300 woman supposed to come on time to the appellplatz, you know, to be counted and to report. And the-- the beds were-- the bridges-- you know, there were three bridges-- three-- one, two, three-- that they were smooth, they were in order, nobody should-- that-- that-- that was my job.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the conditions like in Plazsow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrible. Horrible. We got a little bit of coffee and a piece of bread in the morning. And in noontime, we got a kettle of water soup, which-- nothing. And in the evening, we got again a piece of bread and again some soup. It horrible, horrible. Absolutely horrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were you given to wear there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we got some-- we got some clothing. We didn't get the uniforms. They were not in the beginning. The uniforms came later.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the sanitary conditions like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrible-- it was latrine and like the soldiers had, you know? There were about a hundred benches with holes, about hundred, one next to the other-- terrible, terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was-- in [NON-ENGLISH], there was a bath where you-- you-- you had to go there. They checked you for lice and for, you know, with some [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] smelling thing, they sprayed you that-- oh, terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have to do anything you didn't want to do because you were the block altester?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no-- no way of wanted or not wanted. You just had to do it, what you had to do, that's all. You want it, or you don't want it. Some woman, there were workplaces. The woman went to work in the morning. Some was tailors, shoemakers, knitters, furriers, stove workers, all kind of different jobs, you know. Woman went to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a specific job there besides being in charge?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I only was in the block. I only had to take care of the block, that the windows should be clean, the floor should be clean, it should-- the bridges should be in order. That was my job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any women confide in you at that time or ask you for favors or for things because you were in charge?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they did. They did.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of things did they ask you?

speaker: interviewee
text: What kind of things did they ask you? That their-- their friends should be able to come in at night, that I should hide some children, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] different kind of favors.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever able to do any of the favors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was. I was able to do it. And I did it, because I always thought that somehow my-- my husband always was optimistic. And I thought that the-- the war is not going to take forever. And I did. I did. I was not so successful, but I did. I hided some children before the kinder transport. But they-- they-- they lived through this, but they got-- later on, they got catched anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: You physically were able to hide children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, on-- on top of the third bridges, I could hide them. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You would've gotten in trouble had they found out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, [INAUDIBLE] you bet-- you bet your life I would get in trouble. I got in trouble, one woman run away from me. And one day, I came to the appellplatz, and I had answered 300, and I had only 299. So I got 50 lashes-- 50 lashes. But I didn't get 50 lashes. The-- the policeman, the Jewish policeman, which supposed to-- to do these lashes, he said to me, I have to do it. But I'm going to give you every second one. One he gave it to me, and one he put on his boots. That sounded the-- it had the same sound. You know, the people which were in the next-- there was a special room for this purpose. I had-- I had, maybe two months, I had a sore [? back. ?] I got an infection in there. I was-- got me real good. But I was lucky, because I could have-- I could have been killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think he did that? Why do you think the-- the German helped you out there with the giving himself one on the boot?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was not a German. This was a Jewish policeman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think the Jewish policeman did that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because he was a good man. He was a nice, good man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Do I remember his name? No, no, no. He was a good man. There were good men, there were not good men, you know? I am happy-- I was lucky I-- I met a good man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any contact with [NON-ENGLISH], the head-- the head--

speaker: interviewee
text: Me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, none whatsoever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: During this time, how were you able to see your husband or have contact with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: He came to me. He-- he-- he came to me. Nobody touched him. He was untouchable, my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because he was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because he was privileged by Goth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the conversations you and your husband would have when he came to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I should. We had conversation. We were-- he had to come to-- when he played for him, he had to come up to me. And I have to see him that he is alive. Because this was a murderer, this Goth. He was-- he just had a pleasure of killing people. That was his hobby.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see him kill people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I saw a lot of things. I don't think so you want to listen to that-- you want to listen to that, such a terrible stories. And there is really no-- no-- no way of expressing, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] telling you what I have seen. I don't think so I could. Could I? How could I? Could I? There was a-- there was a time where we had to-- all block altester-- there were also men block altester, which was in the-- in the men blocks. We were called to the appellplatz. It was 20, 10 woman and 10 men. We had to come to the appellplatz, and we had to take shovels, and we had to dig a hole as big as this room, as big as this room, deep hole, we had to dig. After the-- the hole was ready, we had to stay around the hole. It was forced to it. And then there came a-- there came a-- a-- truck with people, with Jewish people. And they came off the truck. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] They had to undress naked. Then they had to be around. They had to go on their knees. They got a shot here in the neck and a stick with their foot, and they fall in the hole. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And after all of them was-- there were maybe 50 of them, men and woman. And after they were in there, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the commandant came. And he said, I wanted you to see that, and I wanted you to tell this to your people in the block that this is going to happen to them when one runs away. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] There-- there were terrible things. There were terrible things. There were people which you just put them in front, and every second, he shot, just-- just for pleasure, not any reason. He was a devil. He was-- he was-- he was not a human being. And he loved music. He loved music. And my husband-- he laid on the couch, and my husband played him lullabies. And he gave him a bottle of schnapps, and he should drink it. He loved beautiful classic Tchaikovsky, and great comfort. Whatever he played. The most beautiful music, he loved it. And he was an expert. He was an expert. My husband had two violins, and he know-- he know exactly when he switched the violin. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A beautiful man. Beautiful looking, tall, handsome man. Beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your husband ever feel as if he was in danger that-- that Goth would change on him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every moment-- every moment he was there, he was in danger. Every moment. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He never knew when he went there that he's going to come alive back. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah. And there where he met Oskar Schindler.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: There he met Oskar Schindler. Oskar Schindler was a guest of-- of Amon Goth.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: He came there to the dinner parties in this villa where he-- where he is living. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he met him. And he loved music, too. He played for him, and then Oskar changed. Oskar-- he was a Nazi, of course. But then he-- he saw once some girls, some-- two young girls run away. And Goth told to the police, when we not going to find these girls, there-- there are going to be 50, or I don't know, people are going to be killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the police did whatever they could, and they found these girls. They found these girls. They brought them back to the appellplatz. Everybody had to come on the appellplatz.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was just the time that Schindler was there visiting. And he hung these two girls. In the middle of the appellplatz, there was-- he hung-- he hung them. And then he shoot them yet, too. And Oskar saw that. He vomited. And I think-- that what my husband said, that he think from there on, he-- he changed. That's what he said later on. He-- he started to work against them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your husband have a conversation with Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, he had many conversations with Schindler, when he had-- many. He-- he gave-- there was a time where everybody had to give whatever little money they had yet. Everybody had to give everything away. Oskar Schindler gave him at this time 500 zlotys. Which was so much there then here $500. So I think my husband, at this time, was the richest man in camp. He had many conversation. And then he-- that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: By the way, during this time, so we don't forget, your son is still with the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my son got back and forth, back and forth. Then there was the time where my-- my-- my-- my brother-in-law's brother was a police chief, a Jewish police chief in Bochnia, which was a little town not far away from Krakow. So Goth told to Henry that there's going to be an action, that he should send away the-- the child.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they put him in a truck which went to work. They covered him. Nobody saw it. And one of-- I think-- took him out of the ghetto and brought him to Bochnia. And he was there maybe three or four months, until something happened in Bochnia. Then he came back to-- So you-- so there was always a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Always action, always action.

speaker: interviewee
text: But there was always somebody telling--

speaker: interviewee
text: Telling.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Your husband what was going to happen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Believe it or not, this murderer, this terrible, terrible murderer, through him, I think I have saved my son. He saved my son.

speaker: interviewer
text: What of Goth-- what was his relationship with Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, no relationship. He was just the guest in his house-- no relationship.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did your husband leave it with Schindler when-- when Schindler left, for instance, to go away? Did-- did Schindler say things to him? Did he give him encouragement?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. No, not yet. Not-- no. That wasn't-- that-- that wasn't so far yet. That came-- that was a lot of time yet to give encouragement.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in Plazsow, from 1942 to--

speaker: interviewee
text: I left Plazsow for Auschwitz in 1944. At the end of 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We'll stop the tape and pick that up on the next one.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
